workInformation.title: Digital T[H]Readings [1994-2025]
workInformation.workDescription:
Digital T[H]Readings [1994-2025] is an interactive browser-native archive-work that treats documentation as a literary form: authored, navigable, and meant to be read rather than merely referenced. Structured as an expandable chronology, it invites viewers to traverse three decades of electronic literature and internet art through dense year-by-year nodes. Each entry pairs core metadata (medium, context, collaborators) with an “About” layer that performs a compact critical narration: how a work functioned in its original technocultural moment, what kinds of networked communities carried it, and what remains when those conditions dissolve.

Where many archives hide breakdown, Digital T[H]Readings keeps it visible. Dead links, vanished hosts, deprecated formats, shifting browser standards, and platform migration are surfaced as part of the record, turning obsolescence into a readable condition rather than a silent technical failure. In the ELO2026 context of (Un)Supervised, the work foregrounds how digital culture is continually “supervised” by commercial infrastructures and automated gatekeeping, while preservation and access-making often happen through unglamorous, human-scale labor: tending, narrating, re-linking, and re-situating.

The interface supports both linear traversal and associative drift, allowing history to be encountered as sequence or recombination. Embedded screenshots and contextual notes operate as micro-traversals, enabling encounter even when original environments no longer run. Digital T[H]Readings thus functions as both archive and artwork: a map of migrating networks and techno-communities, and a model for archiving electronic literature without de-authoring it. It asks readers to notice who gets excluded when access is supervised and how community persists through repair, not scale alone.

workInformation.title: What Happened: Indexed Evidence
workInformation.workDescription:
What Happened: Indexed Evidence is a Twine hypertext memoir structured as the backmatter index of a book that does not exist. The work documents intimate partner abuse through the form of its aftermath: an index compiled in retrospect, in language built after survival rather than during it.

Navigation follows "see also" cross-references, replicating the associative, non-linear logic of trauma. Some paths resolve. Others terminate without exit. An input field accepts text and returns nothing. A second-person interrogation demands to know where you were and who you were with. Twine's linked, nonlinear architecture is the argument. The platform's passage-based structure, in which every node connects outward but no path leads to a recoverable center, formally enacts what the index's content describes: a system of cross-references that circle meaning without arriving at it. The form not only illustrates the experience of surviving abuse but also replicates its logic.

The piece draws on several intersecting frameworks in electronic literature and digital memoir. Scott Rettberg's Fin du Monde, a multimodal work published in The Digital Review combining AI-generated images and music to narrate a post-human apocalypse, introduces cyborg authorship as a framework for understanding how digital processes surface a cultural unconscious. This work grounds the project's treatment of survival language as data: indexed, cross-referenced, and ultimately irrecoverable in its totality.

Christine Wilks' Voices, an interactive digital fiction developed through the "Writing New Bodies" research project and exhibited at ELO 2023, withholds its subject's body entirely, generating meaning through deliberate absence. This work informs this piece's central formal choice that only the effects of what happened are indexed, never the event itself.

Lisa Farrell's "Daemons and Ghosts," an autotheoretical research log published in The Digital Review documenting AI collaboration on fiction, traces the experience of editing oneself into "only a character." That perspective shapes the autoethnographic logic here, where indexing becomes a technology of self-protection.

Laura Okkema's dissertation Hearing the Voices of the Deserters argues that hypertext authors exercise more control than print authors by coding every possible outcome, making constrained navigation a rhetorical force rather than a freedom. Such thinking drives the structural logic of this hypertext: every "see also" returns to the same place, and the reader who believes they are navigating freely is not.

The piece's formal argument is in the replication: abuse is structure. The entry that would explain everything is REDACTED. It was never indexed, but the reader searches for it anyway. The index that could not be written while it was happening is completed, finally, by a stranger clicking through it.

workInformation.title: GAIA
workInformation.workDescription:
Gaia is an interactive narrative that explores the fragility of our reality. It uses procedurally generated content by combining audio, image, and text progressions to ensure every read through is unique while maintaining a core narrative trajectory. A few key decision points lead to different endings. Gaia is available at https://mosher.art/gaia

The work references the lurking feeling that every decision we make leads to a split in reality, spawning an infinite multitude of paths not taken. It asks: what would it be like to navigate those paths? to be able to step through the lost branches of the multiverse? Throughout the narrative these differing entanglements spin in and out of existence creating a kaleidoscope of black and white line drawings. While the interior setting of a recreational vehicle remains consistent, the figures in the scene come and go, their audible chatter mixing with the inner written thoughts of the narrator/reader. Diffuse clouds of text swirl around decision points hinting and future outcomes. The primary mechanic, spinning, offers not so much a decision point but an opportunity to jump between the paths as the wayfinding mechanics are slowly revealed. If you could change a past decision you made, would you? And what would you take with you to help guide your way back to the present?

The public is invited to play through the Gaia narrative. Yellow text indicates clickable links to progress the story. Gaia is written and narrated in English. Average interaction time is 3 minutes.

workInformation.title: Static Sky: And Yet It █████
workInformation.workDescription:
"Static Sky: And Yet It █████" is an experimental video game based on the fear of and resistance to artificial intelligence. It imagines a future ruled by AI world models, where everything is generated by AI, reality is redefined, hometowns are reshaped, and symbols are reoriented.

In this future, the protagonist wears a RV (Real Virtuality) headset that claim to show "reality" within the "virtual." The seemingly real traces revealed by the RV glasses (such as unfinished noise and uncolored white objects) trigger his mental breakdown and fear of reality.

By depicting this collapse and the disintegration of meaning caused by reality, "Static Sky: And Yet It █████" presents a possible future of extreme artificial intelligence. The protagonist's mental symptoms are a hypothesis about the broader human cognition of the future.

This project was made possible by a grant from the Asian Studies Center at the University of Pittsburgh.

workInformation.title: "At the Guggenheim (redux)"
workInformation.workDescription:
This major expansion and revision of "At the Guggenheim" uses CapCut (an AI video generator) to bring the paintings in the Guggenheim Museum to life and tell the unspoken (and secret) story of the tour guide. Unlike a text-based story, our understanding of the action relies on the visual cues within the animated paintings themselves. How do we "read" them? How can we interpret their narrative cues? Can they tell a story as efficiently, if not more so, than a text-based story? Throughout the online tour, we interact with the paintings on our own, without the help of the tour guide, but we are also subtly manipulated by her curation of the art works. We are granted the illusion of individual interpretation but, the whole time, our judgement is being manipulated, and we are witnesses to an unintentional confession. In the end, our tour is, in fact, (un)supervised.
This work uses video effects generated by CapCut (an AI video generator), and then converted into animated gifs using Adobe Photoshop and Adobe Premiere Pro, with additional writing, compiling, and editing using HTML5, JavaScript & CSS. It is playable on desktop, laptop, and portable devices, including phones.

artificialIntelligenceToolsUsed: This work uses video effects generated by CapCut (an AI video generator), and then converted into animated gifs using Adobe Photoshop and Adobe Premiere Pro, with additional writing, compiling, and editing using HTML5, JavaScript & CSS.

workInformation.title: "A Last Goodbye"
workInformation.workDescription:
"A Last Goodbye" contextualizes the traditional elements of storytelling within a new, and dynamic, approach to reader engagement. A massive revision of an older story (originally written in 2024-2025), "A Last Goodbye" has a different title and more fully developed action, conflict, and characters. The interactivity has been improved with a wider and more engaging variety of user interactions, all of which "pull" against, or with, the story events. The visual reinforcement of plot and theme has been enhanced with not only new images and animations, but "broken" and interrupted textual spaces that reflect the distant isolation of the narrator, and the reader's often frustrated attempts to learn his story. This work is a good example of how a narrative can be realized through online multimedia, and how traditional story elements, recontextualized within the affordances of new technologies, still persist in electronic literature.
This work is made with HTML5, JavaScript & CSS and is playable on desktop, laptop, and portable devices, including phones.

workInformation.title: “Talan Memmott: Works on Screen, 1998-2016”
workInformation.workDescription: This online exhibition created by members of the Electronic Literature Lab and curated by Dene Grigar features works of net art by Talan Memmott held by The NEXT that explores the aesthetic of code, language, and code language. Supported by funding from the Society of American Archivists (SAA), the exhibition was conceived when the lab was conducting formal video recordings, called Traversals, of works Memmott had produced on the Shockwave platform and others that were no longer accessible on contemporary computers. In conversation with the artist, it was clear that The NEXT held what may be the only copies of some of Memmott’s net art output dating back to his earliest pieces, including the highly regarded “Lexia to Perplexia.” Along with direct access to many of Memmott’s works, visitors will also find those preserved via Traversals. Additionally, the exhibition features video testimonials, criticism about his work, ephemera, and video and sound recordings of his performances.

workInformation.title: Dreamscapes
workInformation.workDescription:
Dreamscapes is a web-based narrative reflecting on relationships between experience and memory by referencing liminal spaces between dreaming and waking. 
The work begins when the title word is clicked. As new lines of the narrative play out, subsequent lines dissolve, similar to the nature of recalling elements of a dream upon waking and having those same, very real and perhaps felt elements, disappear as consciousness blooms in the waking mind. The narrative and imagery move between waking and dreaming inviting the reader to ponder the tangibility of thoughts, the magic of mind, and the fractures between parsing and knowing.

hardwareDependencies: Mouse or touch capable device with screen

workInformation.title: "Pale Rider"
workInformation.workDescription:
Pale Rider is an isometric role-playing game that will see players take on the role of a Pale Rider near the end of his life hoping to be of help to the world one last time.  Players will navigate a world that, from an artistic and aesthetic perspective, is a blend of the European Medieval and the American Wild West.  Across this Pale-touched alternate world, players will roam across varying environments from untrodden wastes to tense and nerve-racked town, and brave the Pale not only to perform their job of forecasting where this apocalyptic storm is to head next, but also make themselves useful in smaller contexts, aiding those their career would normally see them never coming in contact with, all while battling the effects of decades’ worth of Pale exposure, a deteriorating mental state, and malignant forces acting against the survival of this crumbling world.

Pale Rider is to be built in Unity engine for PC with an emphasis placed on nonlinear storytelling, characterized by branching narratives, story-critical decisions, and character creation through the assignment of stats and use of different clothing and equipment.  Such aims are to be assisted through the use of third-party software, one such being Articy, a storytelling program with Unity integration capable of importing dialogue flows, characters, sound, items, scripts, etc.

hardwareDependencies: A Computer with a working graphics card
softwareDependencies: Web Browser for installing the game
peripheralDependencies: Speakers to experience dialogue, music, and sound effects

workInformation.title: Interferography
workInformation.workDescription:
'Interferography'' is an interactive digital artwork exploring an imaginary visual language. The 'language' is created from microscopic imagery of virus fragments digitally rendered in 3D space; three layers of imagery for each digital colour channel of red, green and blue. The project combines real-time 8K 3D digital imaging and face-tracking, calculating the physical location and orientation of a viewer's face as it appears in the camera as they observe the work. This information is used to determine the angle of view (parallax), the application of a dolly effect to the three RGB layers of each element (diffraction), and the level of zoom in the imagery. The movement of the viewer thus causes changes in the appearance of the work, creating iridescent moire effects in each visual element, as the layered visual mattrices shift their spatial relationships, evoking how interacting data patterns (as diffracting interferograms) might be interpreted as linguistic signs with dense semiotic potential. Interferometry based imagery is routinely used to encode data (eg: interferograms embedded in credit cards, passports, and some currencies, as well as in holographic imagery). Here the proposition is that the encoding techniques employed in 'Interferography', whilst apparently machine readable in character, have the potential to evoke human-readable language (although, obviously, in 'Interferography' the 'text' actually remains illegible).

'Interferography' is a development of earlier interactive artworks exploring imaginary languages; 'Autography' (2020), 'Corpography' (2023) and 'Neurography' (2024); and a series of artworks, including 'Data Double' (2022), 'Correlates' (2023) and 'Diffusion' (2025), that explored the visual diffraction of viewers as irridescent (data) 'subjects'.

Technical specification: Macbook Pro M3 Max, OSX 14.4.1, Processing 4.3, Samsung QN800D 8K display, Canon EOS R50 camera with 18-45mm lens. Note that the screen recording is rendered at 4K due to the technical limitations of current screen recording software. There is four times as much information visible in the live artwork.

hardwareDependencies: Macbook Pro M3 Max, OSX 14.4.1, Samsung QN800D 8K display, Canon EOS R50 camera with 18-45mm lens.
peripheralDependencies: Samsung QN800D 8K display, Canon EOS R50 camera with 18-45mm lens.

workInformation.title: Alice in the Forest
workInformation.workDescription:
Alice in the Forest is a Twine-based hypertext novel about dream logic and associative storytelling in which I try my best to play Wittgenstein's Language Game, exploring the possibilities of language and signifiers in general.

Dream logic differs from conventional fiction in that actions are not performed, but thought. One’s train of thought is the greatest guiding force in the direction of a dream. Therefore, instead of having the reader choose which actions to perform, the story is navigated with only words within the text itself linking to other passages. To click on a word is like dwelling on a thought in a dream - soon you'll find yourself riding along that thought towards unknown depths of the dream. 

The two main characters are based on Dodgson and Alice. Charles Dodgson had a very complex relationship with the Liddell family, which serves as groundwork for juxtaposing fiction and reality. It also demands an exploration of the relationship between writer and fiction. Many can construct grand worlds with many characters in their minds, but frequently those characters are just marionettes which act out the author’s fantasies. How, then, will the dynamic between them be, when Dodgson used Alice the girl as a blueprint for Alice the character?

I also took inspiration from the Japanese word for “word” - “言葉”. It contains the kanji for “leaf”, and there is a deep parallel between a word and a leaf. Leaves grow on branches, branches on trees, and trees form forests. Words are individual meaning-making symbols in a grand scheme of intersecting texts, ideas, and ever-shifting semiotic landscape. 

The way these ideas started from a point of intersection and grew into an intertwining narrative is also very similar to the structure of tree branches. The way the story is written inspired the forest metaphor, and the metaphor opened up new ways to write the story. When viewing all the paragraphs and their relationships in the Twine Editor, they form a tree.

workInformation.title: The Flagrant Algorithms Visit the Lives of 19th Century Women
workInformation.workDescription:
Issues of racial bias, cultural bias, employment bias, and copyright infringement are inherent in contemporary AI systems. [1]  And, the role of algorithms in controlling data generated from AI systems is not widely understood. 

In “Bias Amplification in Artificial Intelligence Systems,” Kirsten Lloyd observes that “To-date, business organizations have operated without consequence to develop and implement AI solutions that afford them efficiencies and competitive advantages; this approach is no longer sustainable because of the repeated negative impacts resulting from biased data,” Kirsten Lloyd observes in “Bias Amplification in Artificial Intelligence Systems.” She points out that “The first line of defense against creating AI systems that inflict unfair treatment is to give more attention to how datasets are constructed before operationalizing them”. [2]

Playing on the issue of gender bias, The Flagrant Algorithms is an information artist’s interactive dataset that -- using the words of 19th Century writers as data -- demonstrates how unseen algorithms control output from AI systems. The data that the system consults is always the same, but in response to the question: “What was it like to be a woman in the 19th Century?”, unseen algorithms selected at random by the system determine how the data is output and in the process produce surprisingly different results.   

For instance, algorithm1 generates words at random from all the data; algorithm2 generates only women-written words, and algorithm3 generates male viewpoint written words. However, although the urls are helpful, a public user might not know which algorithm the system activated.   

Although the primary purpose of The Flagrant Algorithms is to demonstrate how access algorithms can alter meaning in datasets, in it's selection of data,  it also questions who trains data selection and suggests why at this point scholars in all fields should be integrally involved in this process, Additionally, , it demonstrates that artists can make their own systems.

The Flagrant Algorithms is available at  https://www.narrabase.netalgorithms/index_fa.html  Further notes about this work are available at https://www.narrabase.net/algorithms/flagrant_algorithms_notes.pdf

Additionally, because sources of data in AI apps could be cited,  a “sources” algorithm that identifies sources of all the data is linked to the work.
---------------------------------------------
[1] Among many other sources, for a recent look from Stanford see Katharine Miller, "Covert Racism in AI: How Language Models Are Reinforcing Outdated Stereotypes" , Stanford HAI, September 3, 2024 Available at https://hai.stanford.edu/news/covert-racism-ai-how-language-models-are-reinforcing-outdated-stereotypes
[2] Kirsten Lloyd, “Bias Amplification in Artificial Intelligence Systems,” Presented at AAAI FSS-18: Artificial Intelligence in Government and Public Sector, Arlington, Virginia, 2018. Available at https://arxiv.org/abs/1809.07842

I

hardwareDependencies: Displays best on a labtop but also displays on smaller or larger screens
softwareDependencies: Web Browser; displays best on Firefox

artificialIntelligenceToolsUsed: Although the work  demonstrate how  artificial intelligence output can be impacted by access algorithms, the entire work is coded by the author (Judy Malloy with HTML/CSS/JavaScriptt) and no commercial artificial intelligence aps were used.
artificialIntelligenceModelsUsed: My generative system was first developed in Terminals,, 1988 (the 3rd file of Uncle Roger)  My work  in this field earned Second Prize in the Neukom Institute for Computational Science, at Dartmouth University’s Turing Test in the Computational Arts.

workInformation.title: Portraits and other Riffs
workInformation.workDescription:
"Portraits and Other Riffs" investigates regeneration and remix through hybrid human-algorithmic composition. The project brings the performative versioning of jazz improvisation—attunement to audience, openness to accident—into dialogue with digital environments that invite recalibration and transformation. Each piece begins with a source text that is reworked through layered procedural processes and deliberate hand-editing, producing poems that exist as interface-driven performances. The pieces can be appreciated visually, but they can also be seen as scripts for oral performance.

"Portrait of Charles Bernstein" draws on audio recordings of the poet, first rendered through uncorrected speech-to-text transcription. From this unstable textual field, a poem is carved by hand under a simple acrostic constraint and presented through an animated display the reader can modulate.

"Acrosticizer for Rachel Carson" extends this method but uses the environmentalist’s The Sea Around Us as corpus. Lyric variations generated through an interaction with the Collage Poetry Generator are hand-stitched into acrostic sequences that refract Carson’s language. Readers choose one to four lyric scripts, which are loaded in the "poetry console," and then make selections to tune how they are revealed on the screen.  

"Riffs and Remediations" also begins witha source text; but it reworks original, early internet poems and a critical essay by the author into a three-component animation shaped by algorithmic permutation.

"Collage Poetry Generator" makes the compositional engine visible, inviting viewers to experiment with the generative logic underlying the exhibition by riffing with texts of their own choosing.

The interactive displays (html, javascript) and Collage Poetry Generator were coded with Claude AI.

genres: ANIMATED  ·  COMBINATORIAL  ·  CONCEPTUAL  ·  DATABASE  ·  GENERATIVE  ·  PROCEDURAL  ·  HYBRID AUTHORSHIP  ·  CONSTRAINT-DRIVEN POETICS  ·  INTERFACE PERFORMANCE  ·  VERSIONING AESTHETICS
